scope.area: tenant routing, tenant controller response flow, and setup-time tenant test template availability

summary.highLevelVerdict: Ready: manager scanning is compatible with the bundled storage adapter, the first tenant scan is awaited before manager readiness, rescans now clear router-local route cache, dynamic route replacement is self-contained, sessionData reaches tenant controllers, tenant app entrypoints and controller caching are present, and setup plus tenant_create provide a meaningful first-tenant scaffold. Missing/problematic: explicit tenant-side failure responses are still flattened into a generic 502 by the engine handoff whenever the tenant process returns { success: false, status, body }. Optional follow-up: more tenant-specific smoke coverage would help prove missing-controller and invalid-handler behavior end to end.

managerScanAndReadiness.question: Can the manager now scan tenant folders, build route metadata, and become ready only after the first scan completes?
managerScanAndReadiness.answer: Yes. Manager bootstrap explicitly awaits tenancyRouter.scan(), checks the readiness snapshot before declaring itself ready, and the default tenancy adapter rebuilds alias and host maps from the configured tenants root using the bundled storage adapter's Dirent shape. The router-local cache is also cleared on each successful scan cycle now, which closes the stale-cache concern from the previous report.

evidence.note: Manager awaits tenancyRouter.scan(), validates readiness, and only then registers tenancy and session RPC handlers.
Successful scan cycles now clear local cache, mark readiness, and the first scan is awaited before scan() returns.
The adapter scans domains and hosts from the configured tenants root and rebuilds alias and host maps cleanly.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The first runtime request is guaranteed to see a completed tenant scan
The default tenancy adapter is compatible with the bundled storage entry shape
Router-local route cache is cleared when scans rebuild metadata

routeCompilationAndMatching.question: Can tenant routes now be compiled and matched into concrete route metadata without hidden globals?
routeCompilationAndMatching.answer: Yes. The default tenancy adapter compiles routes during scan, route matching is host-aware and falls back to www.<domain> when needed, and dynamic placeholder replacement is now handled locally in tenant-routes-find-match instead of depending on an external global replacer. The bundled tenant template uses redirect, templated asset, session, and wildcard controller routes directly on top of that model.

evidence.note: Compiled routes are attached to routeDataObject during scan.
Route matching uses host-aware lookup and default-host fallback before matching compiled routes.
Dynamic placeholder replacement is self-contained within the tenancy utility.
The default tenant scaffold expresses redirect, templated asset, session, and wildcard controller routes.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Dynamic placeholder replacement is self-contained in the tenancy utilities
The bundled scaffold exercises the current route model with both asset and controller patterns

engineToTenantControllerHandoff.question: Does the engine hand off tenant controller work and preserve tenant-intended failure semantics cleanly?
engineToTenantControllerHandoff.answer: Only partially. The handoff now sends tenantRoute, requestData, and sessionData to the tenant process, and structured success responses can still populate status, headers, cookies, and body. However tenant bootstrap now uses explicit failure response objects with success: false for missing controllers, invalid handlers, and load failures, while tenant-controller-stage treats any success: false result as a generic 502 Bad Gateway. That means tenant-intended 404 or 500 semantics are currently flattened at the engine boundary.

evidence.note: The engine now forwards sessionData with the tenant controller RPC request.
Any response with success === false is converted into a generic 502 Bad Gateway.
Tenant bootstrap returns explicit failure response objects for missing controllers, invalid handlers, and load failures.
Tenant failure responses are intentionally structured with status, body, and success: false.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Fetched session payload reaches tenant controller execution
Tenant bootstrap can produce explicit structured failure responses locally
The engine preserves tenant-provided failure status/body instead of flattening them into a generic 502

tenantProcessControllerExecution.question: Inside the tenant process, is controller loading and invocation now structured clearly and predictably?
tenantProcessControllerExecution.answer: Yes overall. Tenant app bootstrap supports host/app/index.js entrypoints, caches controller modules by resolved path, resolves explicit call handlers when present, and returns structured local failure responses for invalid handlers and load failures. This is a meaningfully more explicit tenant execution model than the earlier generic RPC-failure path.

evidence.note: Tenant app entrypoint loading is part of the runtime bootstrap.
Controller loading, handler resolution, execution, and normalized local failure responses are all handled in the tenant listener.
Entrypoint boot handler resolution, controller caching, and path resolution are explicit helper paths.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Tenant app entrypoint support exists through host/app/index.js
Invalid handler and controller load failures are normalized locally into explicit response objects

setupAndTenantScaffold.question: Do setup and tenant_create still provide a meaningful first-tenant scaffold for the current route model?
setupAndTenantScaffold.answer: Yes. setup.sh still offers an optional first-tenant scaffold step, tenant_create points at the tenant-minimal template and applies ownership plus ACL policy, and the bundled host template exposes redirect, asset, session, and wildcard-controller routes that are compatible with the current runtime.

evidence.note: Setup includes the optional first-tenant scaffold step in the installation flow.
tenant_create points at the tenant-minimal template.
tenant_create creates the host structure, copies the template, and applies runtime ACL policy.
The host template exposes the current runtime's redirect, asset, session, and wildcard-controller patterns.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The installer can offer a first tenant scaffold during setup
tenant_create applies the current tenant template and filesystem policy
The bundled template meaningfully exercises the current route model

mustFix: Preserve tenant-provided failure status/body across the engine handoff instead of converting every success:false response into a generic 502 Bad Gateway.
shouldFix: Add tenant smoke coverage for missing-controller, invalid-handler, and controller-load-failure cases so the intended 404/500 semantics are verified end to end.
niceToHave: Expose richer tenant readiness and route-scan diagnostics for easier runtime inspection after rescans.
